1: Psalm 84 is one of the finest poems in the entire Psalter — a pilgrim's psalm from the sons of Korah, set to the gittît melody (Ps 8; 81). מַה-יְּדִידוֹת מִשְׁכְּנוֹתֶיךָ יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת (mah-yĕdîdôt mishkĕnôtêkā yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt, "How lovely are your dwelling places, O LORD of hosts!") — yĕdîdôt: loveliness, belovedness (the plural abstract noun from yādîd: beloved/lovely — the same root related to love/affection). Mishkĕnôtêkā: your dwelling places (plural of the mishkān — the Tabernacle/Dwelling: Num 24:5). The exclamatory mah (how!) frames the opening as a cry of wonder, not a description. The Psalmist has not yet arrived — still on the road, burning with longing for a place he loves more than anywhere on earth.
2: נִכְסְפָה וְגַם-כָּלְתָה נַפְשִׁי לְחַצְרוֹת יְהוָה (niksĕpāh wĕgam-kāltāh napshî lĕḥaṣrôt yĕhwāh, "my soul longs, yes, faints for the courts of the LORD!") — two verbs of extreme desire: kāsap (niphal: to pale/grow pale with longing — the whitening of intense desire: Gen 31:30: "you longed intensely for your father's house"); kālāh (to be consumed, to fail — Ps 119:81: "my soul kāltāh for your salvation"). The soul is consumed with longing until it fails. לִבִּי וּבְשָׂרִי יְרַנְּנוּ אֶל-אֵל חָי (libbî ûbĕśārî yĕrannĕnû ʾel-ʾêl ḥāy, "my heart and my flesh cry out for the living God!") — the whole person (heart = inner; flesh = body) crying simultaneously. ʾÊl ḥāy: the living God (Ps 42:2; 2 Kgs 19:4,16).
3: גַּם-צִפּוֹר מָצְאָה בַיִת וּדְרוֹר קֵן לָהּ (gam-ṣippôr māṣĕʾāh bayit ûdĕrôr qên lāh, "even the sparrow has found a home, and the swallow a nest for herself!") — ṣippôr: small bird/sparrow (the most ordinary, inexpensive bird — Matt 10:29: "are not two sparrows sold for a penny?"); dĕrôr: the swallow or swift (a migratory bird that travels thousands of miles). Both birds have found their home; the Psalmist is still on the pilgrim road. The irony is tender and painful: the least of birds nests in the sanctuary; the Psalmist who loves it most cannot yet be there. אֲשֶׁר-שָׁתָה אֶפְרֹחֶיהָ אֶת-מִזְבְּחוֹתֶיךָ (ʾăsher-shātāh ʾephrōḥêhā ʾet-mizbĕḥôtêkā, "where she may lay her young — even your altars, O LORD of hosts, my King and my God!") — the bird nests at the very altars: the holiest point of the sanctuary. The contrast between the bird's effortless belonging and the Psalmist's aching distance is the psalm's central tension.
4: אַשְׁרֵי יוֹשְׁבֵי בֵיתֶךָ עוֹד יְהַלְלוּךָ סֶּלָה (ʾashrê yôshĕbê bêtekā ʿôd yĕhallĕlûkā selāh, "Blessed are those who dwell in your house; they are ever singing your praise! Selah.") — the first of three ʾashrê beatitudes in Psalm 84 (vv4, 5, 12). ʾAshĕrê: "how blessed!" — the plural of ʾōsher (blessing/happiness). The Levites and priests who lived in the temple courts: yôshĕbê bêtekā (those who dwell in the house). They are ever (ʿôd: still, continuously) praising. The Psalmist on the road looks at permanent residents and pronounces them blessed. The beatitude structure sets up the psalm's progress: dwellers (v4), pilgrims (v5), trusters (v12). Selāh.
5: אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם עוֹז-לוֹ בָךְ מְסִלּוֹת בִּלְבָבָם (ʾashrê ʾādām ʿōz-lô bāk mĕsillôt bibbābām, "Blessed is the man whose strength is in you, in whose heart are the pilgrim roads!") — the second ʾashrê: not the temple-resident but the active pilgrim. ʿŌz lô bāk: whose strength is in you (Ps 46:1 — not in his own stamina). Mĕsillôt bibbābām: "pilgrimage roads are in their heart" — mĕsillāh: a raised highway (Isa 40:3: "prepare the way of the LORD, make straight a highway"). The roads exist first in the interior: the longing for Zion creates the pilgrimage. The Psalms of Ascent (120-134) are the liturgical expression of those interior roads.
6: עֹבְרֵי בְּעֵמֶק הַבָּכָא מַעְיָן יְשִׁיתוּהוּ (ʿōbĕrê bĕʿêmeq habbākhāʾ maʿyān yĕshîtûhû, "as they pass through the valley of Baka, they make it a place of springs!") — ʿêmeq habbākhāʾ: the "valley of Baka" — bākhāʾ may refer to a balsam/mastic tree (2 Sam 5:23-24; the trees in whose rustle David heard God's movement) or be related to bākāh (to weep) — "the valley of weeping/tears." Both readings are defensible. The transformation: they make it a spring — yāshat: to establish. The weeping valley becomes a spring by the pilgrims' passing through. גַּם-בְּרָכוֹת יַעְטֶה מוֹרֶה (gam-bĕrākhôt yaʿṭeh môreh, "the early rain also covers it with pools!") — môreh: the early October-November rain (Joel 2:23).
7: יֵלְכוּ מֵחַיִל אֶל-חָיִל (yêlĕkû mêḥayil ʾel-ḥāyil, "they go from strength to strength!") — ḥayil: strength, valor (Ruth 2:1; 3:11: "a woman of valor"). From ḥayil to ḥayil: each stage of the pilgrimage depletes and then replenishes — the pilgrim does not weaken but strengthens with each day toward Zion. The movement is paradoxical: the difficult valley (v6) produces not weakness but deeper resources. יֵרָאֶה אֶל-אֱלֹהִים בְּצִיּוֹן (yērāʾeh ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣiyyôn, "each one appears before God in Zion!") — rāʾāh (niphal: the cultic term for appearing before the divine presence — Exod 34:23: "three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD").
8: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת שִׁמְעָה תְפִלָּתִי (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt shimmĕʿāh tĕpillātî, "O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer!") — the fullest possible divine title (YHWH + ʾĕlōhîm + ṣĕbāʾôt — as in Ps 80:19): invoked as the pilgrim makes his mid-journey petition. הַאֲזִינָה אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב סֶלָה (hāʾăzînāh ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb selāh, "give ear, O God of Jacob!") — Jacob who walked the roads from Beersheba to Haran and from Haran to Peniel — the pilgrim-patriarch is the guarantor of God's ear for traveling worshipers. Selāh.
9: רְאֵה אֱלֹהִים מָגִנֵּנוּ (rĕʾêh ʾĕlōhîm māginnênû, "Behold our shield, O God!") — māgên: shield (the divine covering — Gen 15:1: "I am your shield"; Ps 3:3; 18:2). וְהַבֵּט פְּנֵי מְשִׁיחֶךָ (wĕhabbêṭ pĕnê mĕshîḥekā, "look on the face of your anointed!") — mĕshîaḥ: anointed one (the Messiah — the anointed king consecrated by oil: 1 Sam 16:13; 2 Sam 22:51; Ps 2:2; 132:10). Since the whole people is making pilgrimage to appear before God (v7), the prayer for the king is the prayer for the nation's representative. The divine looking at the anointed one's face is the counterpart to the divine face shining on Israel (Ps 80:3,7,19).
10: כִּי טוֹב-יוֹם בַּחֲצֵרֶיךָ מֵאָלֶף (kî ṭôb-yôm baḥăṣêrêkā mêʾālep, "for a single day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere!") — the comparison is superlative: not "better than a thousand bad days" but "better than a thousand days anywhere else." The arithmetic of divine presence upends all human calculation. בָּחַרְתִּי הִסְתּוֹפֵף בְּבֵית אֱלֹהַי (bāḥartî histôpêp bĕbêt ʾĕlōhay, "I would rather stand at the threshold of the house of my God!") — histôpêp: to stand at the threshold, to be a doorkeeper (from sap: threshold/sill). The lowest status role in the sanctuary — simply standing at the door — preferred to comfort in the wicked's tents. מִדּוּר בְּאָהֳלֵי-רֶשַׁע (middûr bĕʾōhŏlê-reshaʿ, "than dwell comfortably in the tents of the wicked!") — even the permanent residence of the wicked cannot compete with the lowest post at the divine door.
11: כִּי שֶׁמֶשׁ וּמָגֵן יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (kî shemesh ûmāgên yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm, "for the LORD God is a sun and shield!") — shemesh (sun) is a rare divine epithet — God is more commonly likened to light, fire, and glory than to the sun itself (the solar disk was a pagan worship object: Ezek 8:16 — men with their backs to the temple worshipping the sun). The epithet is deliberately adopted against this background: the sun-worshippers' object of veneration is, in fact, YHWH's function — warmth, light, orientation, life. The pairing with māgên (shield) covers both positive provision (light/warmth) and protective covering. לֹא יִמְנַע-טוֹב לַהֹלְכִים בְּתָמִים (lōʾ yimnaʿ-ṭôb lahōlĕkhîm bĕtāmîm, "no good thing does he withhold from those who walk with integrity!") — tāmîm: wholeness, blamelessness (from tāmam: to be complete — the walk not split between YHWH and idols).
12: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם בֹּטֵחַ בָּךְ (yĕhwāh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾashrê ʾādām bōṭêaḥ bāk, "O LORD of hosts, blessed is the one who trusts in you!") — the third and climactic ʾashrê beatitude of the psalm. The progression: v4 blessed are the dwellers (in the presence); v5 blessed is the pilgrim (on the way); v12 blessed is the truster (anywhere). The third beatitude is the most expansive: any ʾādām (any person) whose posture is trust (bāṭaḥ: to lean upon, to place one's weight on — Ps 22:4-5; Prov 3:5). The psalm that began with extraordinary longing for the place of God's presence ends by locating blessing in the relationship of trust — a move from physical presence to interior posture.
